retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Post-Transcription Modification and Drug Candidates
retrieved_docs.content: Glycosylation, the enzymatic process by which sugars are attached to proteins or lipids, plays a significant role in modulating epigenetic modifications—heritable changes in gene expression that do not involve alterations to the underlying DNA sequence. O-GlcNAcylation is a notable form of glycosylation that plays a crucial role in epigenetic regulation. Androgen-repressed lncRNA LINC01126 drives the growth of castration-resistant prostate cancer by regulating the switch between O-GlcNAcylation and phosphorylation of the androgen receptor. Antisense oligonucleotides inhibit the growth of castration-resistant prostate cancer cells and have the potential to be developed into a therapeutic drug. [66]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Besides the most important function of RNA to make proteins, other critical cellular functions include modifying and restructuring other RNAs and regulating gene expressions during growth and development, and changing cellular environments.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Replication and Transcription -- Molecular Level -- Transcription
retrieved_docs.content: Once synthesized, the RNA undergoes post-transcriptional modification to prevent its degradation during its exit from the nucleus to the cytoplasm where it will be translated to protein. The single-stranded RNA receives a 5' capping by 7-methylguanosine, which is often an mRNA sequence, as well as the addition of poly-A-tail on the 3' end. During these modifications, eukaryotic cells also undergo splicing, in which portions of the RNA called introns are cut out, leaving only the required bases for translation, known as exons. The newly synthesized RNA strand then travel through the pores in the nuclear envelope to the cytosol in order to participate in protein synthesis by the process known as translation. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The primary function of RNA is to create proteins via translation. RNA carries genetic information that is translated by ribosomes into various proteins necessary for cellular processes. mRNA, rRNA, and tRNA are the three main types of RNA involved in protein synthesis. RNA also serves as the primary genetic material for viruses. Other functions include RNA editing, gene regulation, and RNA interference. These processes are carried out by a group of small regulatory RNAs, which include small nuclear RNA, microRNA, and small interfering RNA.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Fundamentals -- mRNA
retrieved_docs.content: mRNA is transcribed from DNA and contains the genetic blueprint to make proteins. Prokaryotic mRNA does not need to be processed and can proceed to synthesize proteins immediately. In eukaryotes, a freshly transcribed RNA transcript is considered a pre-mRNA and needs to undergo maturation to form mRNA. A pre-mRNA contains non-coding and coding regions known as introns and exons, respectively. During pre-mRNA processing, the introns are spliced, and the exons are joined together. A 5’ cap known as 7-methylguanosine is added to the 5’ end of the RNA transcript, and the 3’ end is polyadenylated. Polyadenylation refers to the process where a poly(A) tail, which is a sequence of adenine nucleotides, is added to the transcript. The 5’ cap protects the mRNA from degradation, and the 3’ poly(A) tail contributes to the stability of mRNA and aids it in transport. Researchers are also studying mRNA as an anti-cancer treatment due to its ability to modify cells. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Mechanism -- Splicing and Pre-mRNA Modifications
retrieved_docs.content: Splicing occurs within the nucleus. Several steps are catalyzed by large (60S) molecules called spliceosomes composed of small ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs) and splicing factors. These enzymes excise the introns from the mRNA transcript while leaving exons in the transcript alone. Spliceosomes shuffle around these exons, depending on the type of protein that needs to be synthesized. After splicing, the 5′ and 3′ tails are modified to help the translation process. At the 5′ end of the pre-mRNA molecule, GTP reacts with the first nucleotide in the triphosphate group on the 5′ ribose's carbon and form a 5′–5′ triphosphate linkage; the nitrogen-7 of guanine is then methylated to form the 5 ′ caps of the mRNA. At the 3′ end, the sequence AAUAAA is cleaved by an endonuclease so that a Poly(A)-polymerase can add the adenylate residues. After all the necessary modifications are made, the mRNA strand is considered mature and is then translocated to the cytoplasm to begin translation.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: A series of proteins called transcription factors aid RNA polymerases in their transcription function. These factors have several functions, mainly facilitating the binding of RNA polymerase II to the promoter sequence, forming the transcription initiation complex. The initiation complex recognizes the promoter region in the DNA as a signal for the transcription starting point. The consensus sequence TATTAA (called TATA box) is located 25–35 bases upstream of the initiation site, followed by a poly-G/C consensus sequence located 32–38 bases upstream. The transcribed RNA contains multiple segments called exons and introns. Exons are segments containing the encoding sequence for protein synthesis, while introns are intervening segments of nucleotides that are excised from the primary RNA transcript (pre-mRNA) in splicing. Splice sequences are found at the 5′ and 3′ tails of introns marked with the dinucleotide GU at its 5′ end and AG at the 3′ end. Alternative splicing is a mechanism used to create different isoforms of the same protein so that several protein isoforms can be created from a limited repertoire of genes. Alternative splicing is now known to deeply influence biological behavior (sex determination, cell differentiation, and programmed cell death). [3] The mechanism underlying alternative splicing is not fully understood yet, but histone modification signatures correlate with splice site switching via chromatin-binding proteins. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of RNA translation into protein is carried out in three steps: initiation, elongation, and termination. During initiation, a tRNA and ribosome join an mRNA. The start codon, AUG, codes for methionine and will always be the first amino acid in the sequence. After initiation, the ribosome moves along the mRNA in the 5’-3’ direction, reading the codons and binding tRNAs carrying the respective amino acids for which the codons are coding. The sequence of amino acids progressively becomes longer and terminates upon reaching a stop codon. The stop codons are UAG, UAA, and UGA. The polypeptide is then released from the ribosomal complex and modified into an active protein. In eukaryotic cells, translation occurs after transcription, while in prokaryotic cells, transcription and translation occur simultaneously.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Replication and Transcription -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Eukaryotic DNA replication occurs in the nucleus of a cell wherein new DNA is made using the original DNA as a template. The process occurs in three stages-initiation, elongation, and termination. Once the DNA is formed, it undergoes the process of transcription synthesizing messenger RNA, which will then be used to generate proteins. Similar to replication, transcription also follows a three-step process of initiation, elongation, and termination. mRNA formation is followed by post-transcriptional modifications in eukaryotic cells and forms the basis of gene expression.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Three main types of RNA are involved in protein synthesis. They are messenger RNA (mRNA), transfer RNA (tRNA), and ribosomal RNA (rRNA).

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: The most recently elucidated epigenetic mechanism is non-coding RNA-associated gene silencing. A non-coding RNA (ncRNA) is a functional RNA molecule that is transcribed but not translated into proteins. Once regarded as waste of the genome, recent insight suggests the ncRNA molecules harbor a crucial role in epigenetic gene expression and likely account for the great difference in phenotype between species and within human populations despite such similarity in encoded proteins. [6] [18] Notable ncRNA molecules include microRNAs (miRNA) and short interfering RNAs (siRNA), which include less than 30 nucleotides, and long non-coding RNAs (lncRNA), which are 200 nucleotides or longer. Though the full extent of their role in epigenetics is still being determined, there is evidence suggesting that ncRNAs participate in DNA methylation and histone modifications in addition to gene silencing. [20] siRNAs and lncRNAs both have been shown to regulate gene expression by the formation of heterochromatin. [6] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ribonucleic acid (RNA) is a molecule that is present in the majority of living organisms and viruses. It is made up of nucleotides, which are ribose sugars attached to nitrogenous bases and phosphate groups. The nitrogenous bases include adenine, guanine, uracil, and cytosine. RNA mostly exists in the single-stranded form, but there are special RNA viruses that are double-stranded. The RNA molecule can have a variety of lengths and structures. An RNA virus uses RNA instead of DNA as its genetic material and can cause many human diseases. Transcription is the process of RNA formation from DNA, and translation is the process of protein synthesis from RNA. The means of RNA synthesis and the way that it functions differs between eukaryotes and prokaryotes. Specific RNA molecules also regulate gene expression and have the potential to serve as therapeutic agents in human diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function -- MicroRNA
retrieved_docs.content: MicroRNAs (miRNA) are non-coding RNAs mainly involved in gene regulation. They are mostly processed from introns and are transcribed into primary miRNA from the host gene by RNA polymerase II. They are then modified by endonucleases, such as Drosha and Dicer, into mature miRNA. Studies have shown that miRNAs that bind to an untranslated region (3’UTR) on mRNAs suppress translation, while miRNA binding to promoter regions can upregulate transcription. [13] miRNAs can also function similarly to hormones. They are released into the extracellular fluid and taken up by target cells for regulation of cellular activity. Additionally, researchers are studying these extracellular miRNAs as ideal biomarkers for various diseases. Research has already shown circulating miRNAs to be involved in cancer through their role in controlling oncogenes and tumor suppressors. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to histone changes, DNA methylation is an epigenetic mechanism associated with gene silencing when the methylation occurs in CpG islands of promoter sequences. [5] Further, non-coding RNA sequences have shown to play a key role in the regulation of gene expression. [6] These epigenetic modifications can be induced by several factors including age, diet, smoking, stress, and disease state. [7] [8] Epigenetic modifications are reversible, but they rarely remain through generations in humans despite persisting through multiple cycles of cell replication. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, DNA Packaging -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The tight structure of chromatin brings about the problem of accessibility to the DNA by enzymes involved in DNA replication and transcription. Chromatin exists in one of two states: heterochromatin, which is condensed and allows little access by transcription enzymes, and euchromatin, which is loose to allow for interaction with transcription enzymes. The transition between these two states is determined by interactions between the DNA and histone proteins via post-translational modifications to the histone proteins like methylation and acetylation. [6] Methylation generally increases interactions between the DNA and histone, thus suppressing gene expression, whereas acetylation will loosen interactions resulting in greater access by transcription enzymes resulting in increased gene expression. [7] The post-translational modifications to histone proteins underlie the mechanisms of epigenetics, which are defined as alterations to gene expression without changes to the DNA sequence. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: RNAP’s function is to transcribe DNA into RNA. The template stranded DNA is read from 3’ to 5’ fashion while RNA synthesis occurs in a 5’ to 3’ manner. Many different kinds of RNA get produced: mRNA, tRNA, rRNA, sRNA, snoRNA, and other noncoding RNAs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1593
retrieved_docs.content: biosynthetic pathways related to protein and muscle growth. Finally, trisaccharides, as short carbohydrate chains, can serve as substrates or regulatory sugars that modulate glycosylation and other carbohydrate‑based biosynthetic processes. Together, these entities illustrate the wide range of chemical, pharmacologic, and biological factors that can define, regulate, or modify biosynthetic mechanisms, underscoring their importance for understanding metabolic regulation and for developing therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1412
retrieved_docs.content: Increased protein synthesis is a physiologic effect associated with both mupirocin and nusinersen. Mupirocin, an antimicrobial agent, and nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, each promote the up‑regulation of protein production as part of their pharmacologic actions, highlighting a shared mechanistic outcome despite their different therapeutic indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1606
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen has a physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis, meaning that treatment with this antisense oligonucleotide leads to reduced synthesis of proteins in the body. This decrease in protein synthesis is a key aspect of mipomersen’s mechanism of action and contributes to its therapeutic and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 116
retrieved_docs.content: oligonucleotide therapy to its broader preparation group. Understanding these classifications, mechanisms (direct versus peripheral action), chemical families, formulation details, and safety relationships is essential for clinicians to select appropriate agents, anticipate therapeutic effects, and manage potential side effects in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionine (S‑adenosylmethionin) and glutamine are both classified as members of the broader chemical group “amino acids and derivatives,” indicating that they share the fundamental structural features of amino acids and related compounds. This categorization reflects their roles in metabolism and biochemistry, as both participate in key physiological pathways typical of amino‑acid‑derived molecules. Recognizing S‑adenosylmethionine and glutamine as amino‑acid derivatives helps clinicians and researchers understand their functions, interactions, and potential therapeutic relevance within the context of amino‑acid metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonukleasy, an enzyme that catalyzes the cleavage of DNA strands, encompasses within its broader class the more specific enzyme streptodornase; streptodornase represents a narrower subclass of deoxyribonukleasy with distinct functional properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212
retrieved_docs.content: Alkylating agents are classified under the category of pharmacological effects—molecular mechanisms, indicating that their therapeutic action is understood within this mechanistic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, including isomerases, ligases, lyases, oxidoreductases, and transferases. Isomerases catalyze intramolecular rearrangements of chemical structures, ligases join two substrates together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a nucleoside triphosphate, lyases cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, oxidoreductases mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, and transferases move functional groups from one molecule to another. Beyond their essential metabolic functions, enzymes also play a central role in pharmacological effects, serving as the molecular mechanisms through which many drugs act, modulate physiological pathways, and influence therapeutic outcomes. Recognizing the classification of enzymes and their involvement in drug mechanisms is crucial for effective drug development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1330
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, each dedicated to a distinct type of chemical transformation. The subclass isomerases (isomerasy) catalyze the rearrangement of atoms within a molecule, converting it into its isomeric form. Ligases (ligasy) facilitate the joining of two molecules with the concomitant hydrolysis of a small pendant group such as ATP, forming new chemical bonds. Lyases (lyasy) break chemical bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often generating a new double bond or a new ring structure. Oxidoreductases (oxidoreduktasy) mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, transferring electrons between substrates. Transferases (transferasy) move functional groups, such as methyl or phosphate groups, from one molecule to another. Together, these enzyme subclasses encompass the primary mechanisms by which cells accelerate and regulate metabolic pathways, underscoring their central role in physiology and therapeutic targeting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 626
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir and cidofovir are both classified as members of the “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors” group. Within this class, ganciclovir’s mechanism of action is identified as “Nucleic Acid Synthesis Inhibitors [MoA],” meaning it exerts antiviral effects by inhibiting the synthesis of nucleic acids. By belonging to the same nucleoside/nucleotide category, both ganciclovir and cidofovir share structural and functional properties that target viral replication through disruption of nucleic acid synthesis, a key principle in antiviral drug therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 397
retrieved_docs.content: Actinomycin is chemically related to depsipeptidy, indicating that it belongs to or is associated with the broader class of depsipeptide compounds. In addition, Actinomycin functions as a DNA minor groove binding agent, placing it within the category of agents that specifically bind to the minor groove of DNA to interfere with transcription and replication processes. This dual characterization highlights both its structural classification as a depsipeptide‑related molecule and its pharmacological role in targeting DNA architecture.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 570
retrieved_docs.content: regulators of biosynthetic pathways. Recognizing these relationships clarifies how antimetabolite and immunosuppressive agents are organized, their mechanistic targets, biosynthetic requirements, and potential for resistance, providing essential insight for patient management, metabolic research, and therapeutic development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: antiparasitic, and supportive pharmacologic agents, highlighting shared mechanisms such as decreased protein synthesis, overlapping chemical families (imidazole, triazole, nitroimidazole), and dual therapeutic roles that are essential for informed therapeutic decision‑making and optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: Unknown Cellular or Molecular Interaction [MoA] is characterized by having the mechanism of action of jodid draselný.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1557
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionin (S‑adenosylmethionine) and betain are both classified as members of the broader biochemical group “Amino acids and derivatives.” In addition, the therapeutic protein metreleptin is also considered a member of this “Amino acids and derivatives” category, reflecting its composition of amino‑acid residues. Recognizing that S‑adenosylmethionin, betain, and metreleptin share this common classification highlights their related metabolic origins and can inform their roles in physiological processes and potential therapeutic use.
